summary: This post demonstrates how to build a distributed random forest algorithm using Dask's task submission interface, applying it to NYC taxi data to predict passenger counts. Rocklin walks through scattering data across a cluster, training independent classifiers on each partition, and aggregating predictions via tree reduction. Despite the sophisticated distributed computing approach, the model achieves only 67% accuracy—worse than simply guessing 'one passenger' every time (70%). The post is refreshingly honest about this failure, emphasizing that the goal is teaching distributed computation patterns rather than winning a machine learning competition. Rocklin concludes that the flexible task submission API (executor.map and executor.submit) is more exciting than high-level collections because it enables arbitrary distributed workflows.
key_points: Dask.distributed's submit/map/gather API enables building custom distributed algorithms beyond the standard bag/dataframe/array abstractions
Tree reduction patterns allow scalable aggregation without overwhelming single machines with data
Replicating data across workers trades memory for communication efficiency, but requires careful management
No amount of distributed computing sophistication substitutes for domain expertise and common sense
The flexible task submission interface offers more freedom than predefined collection abstractions
money_quotes: Our machine learning model does not perform well, but we do learn how to execute ad-hoc computations easily.
This is where my ignorance in machine learning really kills us.
Spoiler alert: we're going to do a lot of computation and still not beat the 'always guess one' strategy.

Still worse than the naive 'always guess one' strategy. This just goes to show that, no matter how sophisticated your Big Data solution is, there is no substitute for common sense and a little bit of domain expertise.
Personally this interface excites me more than collections like Dask.dataframe; there is a lot of freedom in arbitrary task submission.
If you're excited about machine learning you can stop reading here. However, if you're interested in how to build distributed algorithms with Dask then you may want to read on.
key_insight: Distributed computing frameworks provide powerful primitives for building custom algorithms, but computational power cannot compensate for poor problem understanding—domain expertise matters more than scale.
